name: Skinput
short_description: A bio-acoustic sensing armband that turns the skin of the arm and hand into a tappable input surface.
long_description: Skinput is a CHI 2010 research system by Chris Harrison, Desney Tan, and Dan Morris that uses a wearable array of piezoelectric sensors to detect the acoustic vibrations that finger taps produce as they travel through the body, letting any spot on the arm or hand act as a button. The captured bio-acoustic signals are turned into time-independent acoustic features and classified by a trained support vector machine, which localizes taps to roughly five on-arm locations with over 90% accuracy. Paired with a pico-projector that displays a UI directly on the skin, it matters as an early demonstration of always-available, on-body interaction that treats the body itself as the interface rather than a separate screen.
ai_note: A trained support vector machine classifies bio-acoustic features to localize taps, so a real machine-learning component drives recognition, but it is a narrow classifier rather than the core of the system.

images.caption: The Skinput prototype armband with its bio-acoustic piezoelectric sensor array, used to localize finger taps on the arm.
images.alt_text: A sensor-laden armband worn on a person's upper arm, with a projected interface shown on the forearm below it for tapping.

sources.title: Skinput: Appropriating the Body as an Input Surface
sources.publisher: ACM (Proceedings of the SIGCHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, CHI 2010)

interactions.title: Tap a button projected onto your forearm
interactions.description: A pico-projector paints a UI directly onto the skin and the armband senses which spot you tap, so you press a glowing button on your own arm to trigger an action instead of reaching for a phone.

interactions.title: Dial a number on your palm
interactions.description: With a keypad projected across the hand and fingers, you tap out a phone number on your own skin to place a call, the body standing in for a touchscreen dialer.

interactions.title: Play Tetris and Frogger by tapping your arm
interactions.description: Demonstrations turn the forearm into a game controller — steering Frogger with four-way directional taps and playing Tetris using on-body buttons.

interactions.title: Slide and pinch to move through menus
interactions.description: Beyond discrete buttons, the system reads continuous sliding gestures along the skin to scroll a hierarchical menu and a pinch gesture to go back up a level.

interactions.title: Control it eyes-free using proprioception
interactions.description: Because you always know where your own fingers and arm are in space, you can tap the right location without looking, operating the interface while it stays hidden under a sleeve or in a pocket.

interactions.title: Train the classifier to your own arm
interactions.description: A short training pass has you tap each on-arm location a few times so the support vector machine learns your body's acoustic signature, then it localizes taps to roughly five spots with over 90% accuracy.

interactions.title: Accept a call by tapping your fingertips together
interactions.description: Using the same fingertip-tap vocabulary it already classifies, an always-worn band could let you answer or dismiss an incoming call with a discreet tap of finger to thumb, no device pulled out.

interactions.title: Eyes-free volume and skip on a hidden player
interactions.description: Mapping forearm locations to play, skip, and a sliding volume gesture would let a runner control music by tapping their sleeve, leaning on the proprioceptive, screenless control the technique was built for.
